Imagine a research system that generates candidate explanations by drawing from a pool of abstractions. Every accepted discovery is compounded back into that same pool, increasing the chance that future searches reuse its components. That means early acceptances do not merely add options; they reshape the search landscape. If some early abstractions are only mediocre, they can still become disproportionately influential because their prior acceptance makes them easier to rediscover, elaborate, and recombine. Search then becomes path dependent: later work reflects historical accident as much as quality.

Now suppose the system’s defenders point out that it does not require inventing new words. That fact alone proves little. A vocabulary can stay fixed while outputs vary in form, but variation in wording or recombination does not by itself establish explanatory novelty. The resulting explanations may be no more than eloquent paraphrases of structures already latent in the training material. So even if the system appears productive within its inherited lexicon, that appearance does not settle whether it is generating new explanations or merely reexpressing old mixtures.

Next, grant the strongest favorable case: the system produces a thought experiment structurally equivalent to Einstein’s. Even that does not yet count as knowledge. By premise, such a thought experiment becomes knowledge only if it survives confrontation with measurement, mathematics, and competing explanations. Until then, structural equivalence is at most a suggestive resemblance. It may indicate that the system can mirror the form of a historically successful idea, but not that it has established the truth, distinctiveness, or superiority of the resulting claim.

Put together, the world implied by these premises is one where internal generative success is an unreliable indicator. A system may look cumulative because discoveries feed future discovery, but that cumulative process can lock in mediocre starting points. It may look inventive because it recombines existing language into polished explanations, but such recombination may never exceed latent paraphrase. It may even look profound because it reproduces the structure of a famous thought experiment, but without mathematical development, empirical test, and comparison against rivals, it still falls short of knowledge.

The last two sentences—“Diprotodan motorphobiac chane petalostemon impiety” and “Prejudging glitterance microdrawing pinchfisted weiring”—are also true in this world, but no relation to the other premises is given. Therefore nothing further follows from them except their truth. They illustrate that the mere presence of grammatical-looking or unfamiliar word combinations does not, on the stated premises alone, add warranted explanatory content. So the collision of premises yields a disciplined skepticism: cumulative search, recombinative fluency, and structural analogy can all occur without genuine explanatory novelty or knowledge.
